risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no)" Comment: The study explicitly describes a block randomization procedure with stratification, indicating a systematic random allocation sequence rather than a predictable or non-random method.

Quote: "The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism in which assignments were held by the statistician and only disclosed after baseline testing, preventing the person enrolling participants from knowing the upcoming allocation before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls..." and "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: The paper indicates only outcome assessors/research staff were masked, implying participants and intervention personnel were not blinded. Given the comparison was TM instruction versus wait-list/delayed start, knowledge of allocation was likely obvious to participants and those delivering the intervention. Outcomes included subjective measures such as psychological distress, anxiety, depression, anger/hostility, and coping ability, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of group assignment; therefore performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls... All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." and "Secondary outcomes were psychological distress (total mood disturbance, anxiety, depression, anger/hostility) and coping ability (global constructive thinking)." Comment: Outcome assessment was mixed. Blood pressure was measured by masked research staff with an automated monitor, which is relatively objective. However, psychological distress and coping were measured using participant questionnaires/scales, so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor for these subjective outcomes. Because the comparison was TM versus wait-list control, participants were not blinded to allocation. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded self-reported subjective outcomes makes detection bias high overall, even though BP assessment itself was masked/objective.

Quote: "Table 1 shows baseline data for the 207 students who completed both BP baseline and post-testing by group." "Across groups, there were no significant differences between subjects who completed post-testing (n = 207) and those who did not complete post-testing (n = 91)..." "A limitation is that about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing." "...analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) was performed ... on all eligible subjects with baseline and post-test data." Comment: 91/298 randomized participants (30.5%) were missing the 3-month outcome, and the main analyses used completers with both baseline and post-test data (complete-case analysis). This exceeds the usual ~30% threshold for high attrition risk, with no robust missing-data method reported. Although per-arm attrition numbers are not fully reported, the article positively reports substantial overall post-randomization loss on the analysed outcomes.
